walkthrough.title: Split into Two Teams
walkthrough.text: Let's get Codenames Pictures set up. First, split everyone into two teams of roughly equal size and skill. You need at least four players for a standard game — two per team. Each team picks a color: Red or Blue. If you have two or three players, there are special variants we will cover later, but four or more is the classic experience.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Spymasters
walkthrough.text: Each team chooses one player to be their spymaster. The two spymasters sit on the same side of the table, next to each other. Everyone else sits across from the spymasters — these players are the field operatives. The spymasters will be giving clues, and the field operatives will be guessing. This seating arrangement matters because the spymasters need to share the key card stand between them without the field operatives seeing it.

walkthrough.title: Create the 5-by-5 Grid
walkthrough.text: Now grab the deck of codename picture cards. These are the large illustrated cards with surreal, whimsical artwork on each side. Shuffle the deck well — and be sure to flip over half the deck once in a while during shuffling to mix both sides of the cards thoroughly. Then randomly deal 20 cards face up onto the table in a 4-by-5 grid. Each card shows a unique picture that serves as a codename.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Key Card
walkthrough.text: Find the key cards — these are the smaller cards that reveal the secret identities of all the pictures on the table. Randomly choose one key card and slide it into the stand between the two spymasters. Any orientation works — do not think about it, just slide it in. The key card has a grid that matches the layout on the table. Blue squares are Blue Team's agents, red squares are Red Team's agents, pale squares are innocent bystanders, and the one black square is the assassin. Do not let the field operatives see the key card.

walkthrough.title: Determine the Starting Team
walkthrough.text: Look at the four lights around the border of the key card. They indicate which team goes first. The starting team has 9 agents to find on the grid, while the other team has only 8. The starting team will give the very first clue of the game. This one-agent advantage is balanced by the fact that they have more targets to find.

walkthrough.title: Distribute the Agent Cards
walkthrough.text: Now set up the covering cards. There are 8 red agent cards and 8 blue agent cards. Stack the red agents in front of the Red Team spymaster and the blue agents in front of the Blue Team spymaster. This also helps everyone remember which team they are on. There is also 1 double agent card — flip it to show the color of whichever team starts. It becomes that team's ninth agent card for the game. Finally, place the 7 innocent bystander cards and the 1 assassin card in the middle of the table where both spymasters can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Optional: Set Out the Timer
walkthrough.text: There is a sand timer included in the box, but it is entirely optional. The timer is there in case someone is taking too long on their turn. Any player can flip the timer to nudge a slow player into making a decision. You can also use the timer on yourself if you are struggling to come up with a clue. For your first game, we recommend leaving the timer in the box and playing at a relaxed pace.

2-player and 3-player variants exist for smaller groups

Any player can flip it; you can even use it on yourself
Recommended: skip the timer for your first game

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Here is how Codenames Pictures works at a high level. The spymasters know the secret identities of all 20 pictures on the table — which belong to their team, which belong to the opponent, which are innocent bystanders, and which one is the deadly assassin. The field operatives only see the pictures. Spymasters take turns giving one-word clues that relate to one or more pictures on the grid. Their field operatives try to guess which pictures the spymaster means. The first team to identify all of their agents wins. But if anyone touches the assassin, that team loses immediately.

walkthrough.title: Giving a Clue
walkthrough.text: When it is your team's turn, the spymaster gives a clue. A clue consists of exactly one word and one number. The word relates to the pictures your team needs to guess. The number tells your teammates how many pictures on the grid relate to that word. For example, if two of your team's pictures show things related to the ocean, you might say 'ocean: 2.' You are allowed to give a clue for just one picture, like 'castle: 1,' but it is more fun and efficient to try connecting two or more. Getting three or four pictures with one clue is a big accomplishment.

walkthrough.title: Clue Restrictions
walkthrough.text: Your clue must be only one word. You cannot give extra hints, qualifiers, or commentary — no saying 'this might be a stretch' or gesturing at the table. Your clue must be about the meaning of the pictures, not about their position on the grid or physical features of the cards. You must play in English, though commonly adopted foreign words like 'strudel' are fine. You cannot use a word that is clearly part of one of the visible pictures on the table until that picture is covered by a card. The spymaster must keep a straight face at all times and not reach for any cards while teammates are deliberating.

walkthrough.title: Making Contact — Guessing
walkthrough.text: After the spymaster gives a clue, the field operatives discuss what it might mean. They can debate openly, but the spymaster must stay silent and poker-faced. When the team is ready, one operative officially indicates a guess by touching or pointing to a picture on the grid. The spymaster then checks the key card and covers that picture with the appropriate card to reveal its identity.

walkthrough.title: Guess Outcomes
walkthrough.text: There are four possible outcomes when a field operative touches a picture. First, if the picture belongs to your team, the spymaster covers it with your team's agent card. Congratulations — you may keep guessing. Second, if the picture is an innocent bystander, the spymaster covers it with a bystander card. Your turn ends immediately. Third, if the picture belongs to the other team, the spymaster covers it with the opponent's agent card. Your turn ends, and you just helped the other team get closer to winning. Fourth, if the picture is the assassin, the spymaster covers it with the assassin card. The game is over. Your team loses instantly.

walkthrough.title: Number of Guesses Allowed
walkthrough.text: The field operatives must always make at least one guess per turn. If they guess correctly — a picture of their own color — they may continue guessing. They can keep guessing up to the number the spymaster said, plus one extra guess. That bonus guess lets operatives revisit a picture from a previous clue they missed. For example, if the spymaster says 'river: 3,' the operatives can make up to 4 guesses total. Any wrong guess — a bystander, opponent agent, or assassin — ends the turn immediately. The operatives can also choose to stop guessing voluntarily at any time after their required first guess.

walkthrough.title: Turn Flow and Game Flow
walkthrough.text: Teams alternate turns. The starting team (indicated by the key card border lights) goes first. On each turn, the spymaster gives one clue, then the field operatives guess. The turn ends when the operatives guess wrong, choose to stop, or have used their maximum number of guesses. Then it is the other team's turn. Play continues back and forth until one team wins or a team contacts the assassin.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing
walkthrough.text: The game ends when one team has all of their agent pictures covered. That team wins. It is even possible to win on the other team's turn if they accidentally guess your last agent. The game also ends immediately if any field operative contacts the assassin — that operative's team loses on the spot. Tip for spymasters: before saying your clue out loud, always double-check that it does not accidentally relate to the assassin picture.

walkthrough.title: Invalid Clues and Penalties
walkthrough.text: If a spymaster gives an invalid clue — for example, a two-word clue, a clue about card position, or a clue using a visible codename — the team's turn ends immediately. As an additional penalty, the opposing spymaster may cover one of their own agent pictures with an agent card before giving the next clue, giving them a free point. However, if nobody notices the clue was invalid, it counts as valid. So pay attention!

walkthrough.title: Expert Clue: Zero
walkthrough.text: Advanced players can use 0 as the number in their clue. For example, 'feathers: 0' means 'none of our pictures relate to feathers.' This is useful for steering your team away from dangerous pictures — especially the assassin. When the number is 0, the normal guess limit does not apply. Field operatives can guess as many pictures as they want, but they still must guess at least one. This is a powerful tool once your team is comfortable with the basics.

walkthrough.title: Expert Clue: Unlimited
walkthrough.text: Spymasters can also say 'unlimited' instead of a number. For example, 'animals: unlimited.' This is useful when you have multiple unguessed pictures from previous rounds that all relate to a common theme. The disadvantage is that your operatives do not know how many pictures relate specifically to the new clue versus old clues. The advantage is that they may guess as many pictures as they want with no cap. Use this when your team has been building up a backlog of unguessed pictures.

walkthrough.title: Setting Up the Next Game
walkthrough.text: Want to play again? Setup for the next game is quick. Remove all the agent cards, bystander cards, and the assassin card covering the pictures and return them to their stacks. Flip over the 20 picture cards on the table — now they show their other side with completely different pictures — and rearrange them into a fresh grid. Choose a new key card, swap spymasters if anyone wants a turn, and you are ready to go.

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Teams
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with two teams. Red Team has a spymaster (Red Spymaster) and two field operatives (Red Op 1 and Red Op 2). Blue Team also has a spymaster (Blue Spymaster) and two field operatives (Blue Op 1 and Blue Op 2). We will follow both teams through their first few turns so you can see how clues, guessing, and mistakes all play out. Ready? Let's go.

walkthrough.title: The Board Is Set
walkthrough.text: The 20 picture cards are laid out in a 4-by-5 grid. The key card is in the stand. According to the key card border lights, Red Team starts, so Red has 9 agents to find and Blue has 8. The double agent card has been flipped to red, giving Red Team their ninth agent card. Agent card stacks are in front of each spymaster. The 7 innocent bystander cards and 1 assassin card sit in the middle. Both spymasters are studying the key card carefully.

walkthrough.title: Red Spymaster's First Clue
walkthrough.text: Red Spymaster studies the grid. She sees that two of Red Team's pictures both contain elements that relate to fire — one shows a dragon breathing flames and another shows a campfire scene. She says: 'Fire: 2.' This tells her operatives that two pictures on the grid relate to fire. A strong, clear clue connecting two agents right away.

walkthrough.title: Red Team Guesses
walkthrough.text: Red Op 1 and Red Op 2 discuss. They quickly spot the dragon picture — that clearly relates to fire. Red Op 1 touches the dragon picture. Red Spymaster checks the key card, nods, and covers it with a red agent card. One down! The operatives confer again and point to the campfire picture. Red Spymaster covers it with another red agent card. Two correct guesses! They could make one more guess (the number was 2, so they get up to 3), but they do not have a strong lead from a previous clue, so they decide to stop. Smart — do not push your luck when you are unsure.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was a Good Turn
walkthrough.text: Red Team found 2 agents in one turn. That is solid progress. The key was a clear, unambiguous clue. Both pictures obviously related to fire, so the operatives did not have to agonize over their choices. They also showed good discipline by stopping after their two confident guesses instead of risking a third random pick. When in doubt, stop guessing.

walkthrough.title: Blue Spymaster's First Clue
walkthrough.text: Now it is Blue Team's turn. Blue Spymaster looks at the grid and sees three Blue pictures that seem to relate to water — one shows a submarine, one shows a whale, and one shows a rainy cityscape. He considers saying 'water: 3' but then notices that an innocent bystander picture also features a waterfall prominently. He decides to play it safe and says: 'water: 2' — aiming just for the submarine and the whale, which are the most obviously water-related.

walkthrough.title: Blue Spymaster's Mistake #1 — Overlooking the Assassin
walkthrough.text: But wait — Blue Spymaster made an error. He checked for bystanders but forgot to check whether the assassin picture also relates to water. The assassin picture shows a pirate ship on stormy seas. 'Water: 2' could easily lead his team to that picture. This is one of the most dangerous mistakes a spymaster can make. Always, always check the assassin picture against your clue before saying it out loud. Let's see what happens.

walkthrough.title: Blue Team Guesses — Close Call
walkthrough.text: Blue Op 1 and Blue Op 2 discuss. They quickly identify the submarine — obvious water connection. Blue Op 1 touches it. Blue Spymaster covers it with a blue agent card. One correct! Now they debate between the whale and the pirate ship. Blue Op 2 says 'the pirate ship has stormy water...' Blue Spymaster's face goes pale, but he keeps it straight. Fortunately, Blue Op 1 argues for the whale: 'a whale IS water.' They touch the whale. Correct! Blue Spymaster exhales with relief. The team wisely stops at 2. Disaster narrowly avoided — but only by luck.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check the Assassin
walkthrough.text: Blue Spymaster got lucky. If his operatives had touched the pirate ship assassin picture, the game would have ended instantly with a Blue Team loss. The single most important habit for any spymaster is this: before you say your clue, check it against the assassin. Then check it against the opponent's pictures. Then check it against the bystanders. Only after clearing all three checks should you say the clue out loud. Assassin first, always.

walkthrough.title: Red Spymaster's Second Clue
walkthrough.text: Red Spymaster is feeling confident. She sees three Red pictures that could relate to 'music' — one shows a guitar, one shows a record player, and one shows a bird singing on a branch. She says: 'music: 3.' An ambitious clue going for three at once.

walkthrough.title: Red Team Guesses — Turn 2
walkthrough.text: The operatives quickly find the guitar picture. Correct — covered with a red agent card. They see the record player next. Correct again! Now they are less sure about the third. Red Op 2 says 'maybe the bird singing?' but Red Op 1 thinks the picture of a person at a concert is more likely. They touch the concert picture. Red Spymaster covers it with... an innocent bystander card. Oops. The concert picture was a bystander. Red Team's turn ends immediately. They got 2 out of 3, but the singing bird was the third Red agent.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Discuss Before Touching
walkthrough.text: Red Team's mistake was not discussing thoroughly enough. Red Op 2 had the right instinct about the singing bird, but Red Op 1 jumped to a different conclusion. In Codenames Pictures, discussion is your best weapon. Talk through every option before committing to a guess. The spymaster cannot help you once the clue is given, so your team's debate is the only way to sort out ambiguous pictures. And remember — a wrong guess ends your entire turn.

walkthrough.title: Blue Spymaster's Mistake #2 — Too Clever a Clue
walkthrough.text: Blue Spymaster is eager to catch up. He sees two Blue pictures — one showing a clock tower and one showing an hourglass — and thinks of a brilliant connection: 'temporal: 2.' The problem? His operatives are casual gamers, not vocabulary buffs. Blue Op 1 says 'temporal? Like a temple?' Blue Op 2 shrugs. They stare at the grid looking for pictures of temples instead of time-related images.

walkthrough.title: Blue Team Guesses — Confused
walkthrough.text: After a long discussion, the operatives touch a picture showing ancient ruins — they thought 'temporal' meant 'temple.' The ancient ruins are actually a Red Team agent. Red Spymaster covers it with a red agent card. Blue Team just gave Red a free agent! Their turn is over. The clock tower and hourglass sit untouched.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Keep Clues Simple
walkthrough.text: The lesson here is critical: your clue is only as good as your team's ability to understand it. A brilliant connection that your operatives cannot follow is worse than a simple clue for just one picture. Know your audience. If your operatives are casual players, use common, everyday words. 'Time: 2' would have been far more effective than 'temporal: 2.' Save the clever clues for when you know your team can follow your thinking.

walkthrough.title: Blue Spymaster's Mistake #3 — Giving a Two-Word Clue
walkthrough.text: Flustered by the previous failure, Blue Spymaster sees two Blue pictures related to outer space and blurts out: 'outer space: 2.' Immediately, Red Spymaster raises a hand. 'That is two words — invalid clue.' Blue Spymaster realizes his mistake. The rules are clear: clues must be exactly one word. Blue Team's turn ends on the spot, and as a penalty, Red Spymaster gets to cover one of Red Team's remaining pictures with a red agent card for free.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: One Word Only
walkthrough.text: This penalty is harsh but fair. Clues must be exactly one word plus a number. No compound phrases, no two-word descriptions, no extra commentary. If Blue Spymaster wanted to connect space-related pictures, valid options would be 'space: 2,' 'galaxy: 2,' or 'astronaut: 2' — but never 'outer space.' When in doubt about whether your clue is one word, ask the opposing spymaster quietly before announcing it to the group. And remember: if nobody catches an invalid clue, it stands, so both teams should pay attention.

walkthrough.title: The Game Continues
walkthrough.text: Play continues to alternate between Red and Blue. By this point in the game, Red Team has covered 5 of their 9 agents (including the free one from the penalty). Blue Team has covered 2 of their 8. Each turn follows the same rhythm: spymaster gives one word and one number, operatives discuss, then they guess one picture at a time until they get one wrong, choose to stop, or hit their guess limit. As more pictures get covered, spymasters have fewer options and clues often get trickier.

walkthrough.title: Using Previous Clues
walkthrough.text: On Red Team's next turn, Red Spymaster says 'nature: 1' aiming for one specific picture. After correctly guessing it, the operatives remember the singing bird from earlier — the one they missed from the 'music: 3' clue. They use their bonus guess (clue number + 1) to touch the singing bird picture. It is indeed a Red agent! This is the power of the plus-one bonus guess. Operatives should always keep track of pictures from previous clues that they did not get to. Those carry over and can be revisited on later turns.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen a full round of Codenames Pictures in action. You watched Red Team execute a clean two-for-two opening. You saw Blue Spymaster make three common mistakes: overlooking the assassin when crafting a clue, giving a clue too clever for the team to understand, and blurting out a two-word clue. You saw how the plus-one bonus guess lets operatives catch up on missed pictures from earlier rounds. The core loop is simple — one word, one number, guess the pictures — but the tension and strategy make every round exciting.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into your own game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics on giving good clues and guessing efficiently.

Second — reference the Appendix for quick definitions of every role, card type, and game term.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Play
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy on giving clues and guessing
Reference the Appendix for quick definitions of every term
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: The Golden Rule for Spymasters
walkthrough.text: Before you say any clue, run it through a three-step safety check. First, does this clue relate to the assassin picture? If yes, find a different clue — an assassin contact loses the game instantly. Second, does this clue relate to any of the opponent's pictures? Every opponent picture your team accidentally touches gives the other team a free agent. Third, does this clue relate to any innocent bystander pictures? Bystanders end your turn. Only after clearing all three checks should you say the clue. Assassin first, opponent second, bystander third.

walkthrough.title: Start with Safe Two-Clues
walkthrough.text: New spymasters often try to connect three or four pictures on their very first clue. That is risky. Start with a safe, clear clue connecting exactly two pictures. A two-clue that your team gets right every time is better than a three-clue that confuses them. As you get a feel for how your operatives think, you can gradually increase the number. The starting team needs to find 9 agents and the other team needs 8, so consistently hitting twos will win games.

walkthrough.title: Think Like Your Operatives
walkthrough.text: The biggest spymaster mistake is giving clues based on your own associations without considering how your team thinks. You can see the key card — your operatives cannot. They see 20 pictures and have to figure out which ones match your word. Before giving a clue, scan the entire grid from your operatives' perspective. Ask yourself: if I heard this word without seeing the key, which pictures would I look at? If there are misleading pictures that could pull your team toward a bystander or the assassin, find a different clue.

walkthrough.title: Operatives: Trust Your Spymaster
walkthrough.text: As a field operative, your spymaster chose their clue for a reason. If the clue does not seem to match any obvious picture, resist the urge to guess randomly. Talk it through with your teammates. Consider less obvious interpretations — maybe the spymaster is thinking about a secondary element in one of the pictures, or a metaphorical connection. Your spymaster has been staring at the key card and thinking carefully. Give their clue the benefit of the doubt before going rogue.

walkthrough.title: When to Stop Guessing
walkthrough.text: One of the hardest skills in Codenames Pictures is knowing when to stop. After you have guessed the number of pictures your spymaster indicated, you get one bonus guess. Use that bonus guess only if you are confident about a leftover from a previous clue. If you are not sure, stop. Every wrong guess has a cost: at best you hit a bystander and lose your turn, at worst you contact the assassin and lose the game. Stopping early is almost always better than guessing wildly. You will get another turn.

walkthrough.title: Keep Track of Previous Clues
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, operatives should mentally track clues from previous turns. If your spymaster said 'music: 3' and you only found two music pictures, that third picture is still out there. On a future turn, after finishing the current clue's guesses, use your bonus guess to go back for it. Good operative teams build a mental list of 'missed' pictures and systematically clean them up. This is especially powerful in the late game when every agent matters.

walkthrough.title: Pictures vs. Words: Embrace Ambiguity
walkthrough.text: Codenames Pictures adds a unique challenge compared to the word version. Each picture can be interpreted in multiple ways — one person might see a castle while another sees a mountain in the background. As a spymaster, this means your clue needs to be specific enough to point at the right interpretation of the picture. As an operative, stay open to different readings of each image. Do not fixate on one element. When debating a guess, try describing the picture out loud — different teammates may notice different things.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here is a quick checklist of the most common mistakes we see in new Codenames Pictures games. Spymasters: do not give multi-word clues, do not use a word visible on the table, do not forget to check the assassin, do not give overly clever clues your team cannot follow, and do not react when your team is about to guess wrong. Operatives: do not guess without discussing first, do not forget about previous clues, do not push beyond your confidence level, and do not make eye contact with the spymaster while guessing — you might pick up unintentional nonverbal cues that break the spirit of the game.

summary.title: The Golden Rule for Spymasters
bullets: Three-step safety check before every clue: assassin, opponent, bystander
If the clue relates to the assassin, find a different clue immediately
Check opponent pictures second, bystanders third
Only say the clue after passing all three checks

summary.title: Start with Safe Two-Clues
bullets: New spymasters: aim for 2 pictures per clue, not 3 or 4
A reliable 2-clue beats a risky 3-clue every time
Starting team needs 9 agents; other team needs 8 — twos win games
Increase the number only as you learn how your team thinks

summary.title: Think Like Your Operatives
bullets: Scan the full grid from your operatives' perspective before giving a clue
Ask: without the key card, which pictures would I associate with this word?
If a bystander or assassin could be misleading, find a different clue
Your operatives see all 20 pictures — you see the answer key

summary.title: Operatives: Trust Your Spymaster
bullets: The spymaster chose the clue carefully — give it the benefit of the doubt
Consider secondary elements or metaphorical connections in pictures
Discuss thoroughly with your teammates before guessing
Do not guess randomly if the clue seems unclear

summary.title: When to Stop Guessing
bullets: After matching the clue number, use the +1 bonus guess only if confident
Wrong guess costs: bystander = turn over, assassin = game over
Stopping early is almost always better than guessing wildly
You will get another turn — patience wins games

summary.title: Keep Track of Previous Clues
bullets: Build a mental list of 'missed' pictures from earlier clues
Use the +1 bonus guess to revisit them on later turns
Systematically cleaning up missed pictures wins close games
Especially powerful in the late game when every agent counts

summary.title: Pictures vs. Words: Embrace Ambiguity
bullets: Each picture can be interpreted multiple ways — one person's castle is another's mountain
Spymasters: make clues specific enough to point at the right interpretation
Operatives: stay open to different readings, don't fixate on one element
Describe pictures out loud — teammates may notice different things

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Spymasters: no multi-word clues, no visible codenames, always check assassin
Spymasters: don't react when operatives are about to guess wrong
Operatives: always discuss before touching, track previous clues
Operatives: don't make eye contact with spymaster while guessing

walkthrough.title: The Three-Clue Connection
walkthrough.text: Once you are comfortable giving two-clues, the next level is reliably connecting three pictures with one word. This is how you build a lead or catch up from behind. The key is finding a word general enough to connect three pictures but specific enough to avoid bystanders, opponents, and the assassin. Start by grouping your team's remaining pictures by theme. Look for sets of three that share a clear common element. If you can only find a shaky connection for the third picture, drop back to a solid two-clue. A safe two-clue and a safe two-clue will get you four agents in two turns — the same as a three-clue and a one-clue, with far less risk.

walkthrough.title: Negative Space Cluing
walkthrough.text: Advanced spymasters use the zero clue to steer operatives away from danger. Saying 'poison: 0' tells your team that none of your pictures relate to poison — so any picture that looks poison-related must be a bystander, opponent, or the assassin. This is especially powerful when the assassin picture has an obvious theme that could confuse your team. You can also combine negative information with previous clues. After giving 'poison: 0,' your operatives know to avoid poison-looking pictures when revisiting earlier clues. Use zero clues when defense matters more than offense — for example, when you are ahead and need to avoid the assassin to close out the game.

walkthrough.title: The Unlimited Clue
walkthrough.text: When you have a backlog of two or three unguessed pictures from previous rounds, the unlimited clue lets your operatives catch up in one big turn. Say 'animals: unlimited' if you have multiple animal-related agents your team has not found yet. Your operatives can then guess as many pictures as they want. The downside is they do not know whether one, two, or five pictures match — so they must rely on their judgment. Use the unlimited clue in the mid-to-late game when you need a big swing. Combine it with a clear theme that your team has already been primed for through previous clues.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Opponent's Clues
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to the opposing spymaster's clues. If they say 'forest: 2,' you now know that at least two of the uncovered pictures are forest-related agents for the other team. This information helps you as an operative: you should probably avoid forest-looking pictures on your turn. It also helps you as a spymaster: if you know certain pictures belong to the opponent, you can be more aggressive with clues that might otherwise lead to those pictures. Treat the opponent's clues as free intelligence.

walkthrough.title: Tempo and Pacing
walkthrough.text: Codenames Pictures has a built-in tempo advantage for the starting team: they have 9 agents but go first, which usually means they find agents faster. If you are the starting team, maintain your tempo by giving consistent two-clues. If you are the second team with only 8 agents, you may need to take more risks with three-clues to keep up. The team that finds 2 agents per turn will almost always beat the team that finds 1. If you fall two or more agents behind, consider an ambitious three-clue or even an unlimited clue to swing the momentum back.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Strategy
walkthrough.text: When your team is down to their last 2 or 3 agents, the game dynamics shift. The grid has fewer pictures remaining, which means each clue has fewer decoys but also fewer options. Spymasters should focus on one-clues that are absolutely safe — no assassin risk, no opponent agent risk. One safe agent per turn is fine when you are close to winning. The worst outcome is giving a risky clue that leads to the assassin when you only needed two more turns to win. Operatives should also be more cautious in the endgame. If your spymaster says 'tree: 1,' do not get greedy and guess a second picture.

walkthrough.title: Team Calibration
walkthrough.text: The best spymasters develop a mental model of how their operatives think. After a few games with the same group, you learn which connections they will see immediately and which ones they will miss. This calibration is your biggest strategic advantage. If your teammate always notices animals in pictures, lean into animal clues. If another teammate is strong on colors, use color-based connections. Adapt your clue vocabulary to your team's strengths. This is also why rotating spymasters between games is valuable — it lets everyone learn each other's thinking patterns.

walkthrough.title: Misdirection Defense
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the opponent's spymaster will inadvertently give you useful information through their operatives' guesses. If an opponent operative touches one of your agents by mistake, that is a free find for you. But beyond accidents, you can play defense by paying attention to which pictures the opponent is debating. If they are heavily discussing a picture and then decide against it, that picture is probably not theirs — it might be a bystander or even the assassin. Use this information to narrow down your own guesses on future turns.

summary.title: The Three-Clue Connection
bullets: Group remaining team pictures by theme — look for sets of 3
Word must be general enough for 3 but specific enough to avoid dangers
If the third connection is shaky, drop back to a safe 2-clue
Two safe 2-clues (4 agents) = same output as a 3-clue + 1-clue, less risk

summary.title: Negative Space Cluing
bullets: Zero clue ('poison: 0') tells team none of your pictures relate to that word
Steers operatives away from assassin or dangerous pictures
Powerful when ahead and needing to avoid a loss
Combines well with previous clues — operatives can guess freely

summary.title: The Unlimited Clue
bullets: Use 'unlimited' when multiple unguessed pictures share a theme
Operatives can guess as many as they want — no cap
Best in mid-to-late game for a big catch-up swing
Downside: operatives don't know exactly how many pictures match

summary.title: Reading the Opponent's Clues
bullets: Opponent's clues reveal which pictures belong to their team
Avoid those themed pictures on your own guesses
As spymaster, use opponent intel to give more aggressive clues
Treat every opponent clue as free intelligence

summary.title: Tempo and Pacing
bullets: Starting team (9 agents, goes first) has a natural tempo advantage
Maintain tempo with consistent 2-clues if you are the starting team
Second team may need ambitious 3-clues to keep pace
Finding 2 agents per turn almost always beats finding 1

summary.title: Endgame Strategy
bullets: Last 2-3 agents: shift to safe 1-clues with zero assassin risk
One safe agent per turn is fine when close to winning
Worst case: risky clue leads to assassin when you almost had it
Operatives: don't get greedy, match the clue number exactly

summary.title: Team Calibration
bullets: Learn how your specific operatives think over multiple games
Lean into connections your team spots easily (animals, colors, etc.)
Adapt clue vocabulary to your team's strengths
Rotate spymasters between games to learn each other's patterns

summary.title: Misdirection Defense
bullets: Opponent operatives touching your agent = free find for you
Watch which pictures opponents debate and then avoid
A debated-then-rejected picture is likely a bystander or assassin
Use opponent hesitation as intel for your own guesses

entries.definition: One player per team who sits behind the key card and gives clues. The spymaster knows the secret identities of all 20 pictures on the grid. They give exactly one word and one number as their clue each turn. They must keep a straight face and not react to their team's guesses.

All players who are not the spymaster. Field operatives sit across from the spymasters and cannot see the key card. They discuss clues, debate which pictures to guess, and make the official guesses by touching pictures on the grid.
One of the two competing teams. Red Team's agents are indicated by red squares on the key card. They use red agent cards to cover their identified agents.
One of the two competing teams. Blue Team's agents are indicated by blue squares on the key card. They use blue agent cards to cover their identified agents.
The team indicated by the four lights on the border of the key card. The starting team goes first, has 9 agents to find (instead of 8), and receives the double agent card flipped to their color.

Large illustrated cards with surreal, whimsical artwork on both sides. 20 of these are laid out in a 4-by-5 grid to form the playing field. Each picture serves as a codename that the spymaster must connect to clues.

A smaller card that reveals the secret identities of all pictures on the grid. Shows a grid with blue squares (Blue agents), red squares (Red agents), pale squares (innocent bystanders), and one black square (assassin). Placed in the stand between spymasters, hidden from field operatives.
One of 8 cards used to cover pictures identified as Red Team agents. Stacked in front of the Red Team spymaster at the start of the game.
One of 8 cards used to cover pictures identified as Blue Team agents. Stacked in front of the Blue Team spymaster at the start of the game.

A special card that belongs to whichever team starts the game. It is flipped to show that team's color and serves as their 9th agent card, giving the starting team one extra agent to offset their disadvantage of having more agents to find.

One of 7 cards used to cover pictures that are neither team's agents nor the assassin. Represented by pale squares on the key card. When an operative touches a bystander, the turn ends immediately but no further penalty occurs.

A single card representing the most dangerous identity on the grid. Indicated by the black square on the key card. If any field operative touches the assassin picture, that operative's team loses the game instantly.
A physical stand that holds the key card upright between the two spymasters so both can reference it. The stand blocks field operatives from seeing the key card's face.
An optional component included in the box. Any player can flip the timer to encourage a slow player (spymaster or operative) to make a decision before time runs out. Not required for standard play.

The information a spymaster provides on their turn: exactly one word plus one number. The word relates to one or more pictures the team should guess. The number indicates how many pictures on the grid relate to that word. Example: 'ocean: 2.'

When a field operative officially touches or points to a picture on the grid. The spymaster then checks the key card and covers the picture with the appropriate identity card. Each guess reveals one picture's secret identity.

After guessing correctly a number of times equal to the clue number, operatives are allowed one additional guess. This extra guess lets them revisit a picture from a previous turn's clue. Maximum guesses per turn = clue number + 1.

The act of an operative touching a picture on the grid to make an official guess. 'Making contact' reveals the picture's secret identity. Correct contact with your team's agent allows continued guessing.

One team's complete sequence of play: the spymaster gives one clue, then the field operatives make their guesses. A turn ends when operatives guess wrong, choose to stop, or exhaust their maximum guesses (clue number + 1).

A clue that violates the rules — such as using more than one word, referencing a visible codename, describing card position, or using the clue number as part of the clue. An invalid clue ends the team's turn immediately, and the opposing spymaster may cover one of their own agents for free as a penalty.

An expert-level clue where the spymaster uses 0 as the number (e.g., 'feathers: 0'). This means none of the team's pictures relate to that word, helping operatives avoid dangerous pictures. The normal guess limit does not apply — operatives can guess as many as they want but must guess at least one.

An expert-level clue where the spymaster says 'unlimited' instead of a number (e.g., 'animals: unlimited'). Useful when multiple unguessed pictures from previous rounds share a theme. Operatives may guess as many pictures as they want with no cap.
The 4-by-5 arrangement of 20 codename picture cards on the table. The grid layout corresponds to the pattern on the key card. Pictures remain in their grid positions until covered by identity cards.
When a picture's identity is revealed, the spymaster places the appropriate card (agent, bystander, or assassin) on top of it. Covered pictures are no longer in play and cannot be guessed again.

A core spymaster responsibility. The spymaster must not react — positively or negatively — to their team's discussion or guesses. They should not reach for any card while operatives are deliberating. Operatives should avoid making eye contact with the spymaster to prevent picking up unintentional nonverbal cues.

A cooperative variant for two or more players on the same team. One player is spymaster, the rest are operatives. The opponent team has no players — the spymaster simulates them by covering one enemy agent each time the opponent would take a turn. Score is based on how many enemy agents remain when you win.

Two players act as competing spymasters and the third player is a shared field operative working for both teams. Setup and gameplay follow normal rules, but the single operative guesses for whichever team's turn it is. The winning spymaster wins; the operative tries to do well for both sides.
